learning_outcomes.description: To produce a self-directed project consisting of original audio-visual work
To show verbal and written understandings of key socio-historical influences upon media technologies as platforms and devices
To show verbal and written understandings of advanced media theory

assessments.task_description:
In this course, workshops are designed to support your learning and actively scaffold assessment tasks. Eight (8) workshops will include a set of in-class activities relating to the weekly topic, reading and lecture, and will involve reflective writing, responding to questions about course content, and practical making tasks to be completed in the workshop (requiring the use of Adobe software). These tasks will require critical thinking and prior preparation through attending lectures and engaging with course readings and resources. These activities and responses, unless necessarily different due to the nature of the task, will be handwritten and the use of machine translation will not be permitted. They are worth 5 marks each, with a total of 30 marks available. The best 6 workshops of the 8 available workshops will be counted towards your grade.

Because only 6 of the 8 count, the assessment already builds in the flexibility to miss up to 2 sessions — for illness or any other reason — with no penalty and no documentation required. These must be completed in class.

There are no opportunities to re-take an individual exercise, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below. As this is an internal course, you must make yourself available to complete each exercise at the scheduled date, time and venue. Unless otherwise directed, these are handwritten, closed-book tasks: no written material, electronic material or electronic devices may be used. All such items must remain in your bag or under your desk, and all electronic devices must be switched off or in airplane mode. If a device emits any noise, you may be subject to an allegation of misconduct and the item may be confiscated for the duration of the exercise. You may bring suitable writing implements (in a clear container), water in a clear bottle with the label removed, and disposable earplugs.

Alternative assessment and accommodations

If you have a Student Access Plan relevant to this task, please contact the course coordinator as early as possible. Reasonable accommodations can be made for matters documented in a Student Access Plan (for example, if you are unable to handwrite under timed conditions).

The 2-session buffer described above is intended to cover ordinary absences. A separate make-up session is available only to students who, for a genuine and documented reason, miss more than 2 of the exercises — for example, a sudden or serious illness, or another documented cause requiring a lengthy absence. Missing 2 or fewer sessions is already covered by the best-6-of-8 structure and does not qualify for a make-up.

To be considered for a make-up session or any other alternative arrangement, you must contact the course coordinator with your documentation as soon as possible after the circumstances arise.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
How do digital media technologies shape society? How are they in turn shaped by society? For the Media Proposal and Design Draft, you will propose a speculative media technology that will allow you to explore these driving questions.

You will research, conceptualise and design a speculative media technology or platform that is plausible as a digital media technology of the near future. The idea and design of the media technology should respond to a social, political or cultural issue, and must be informed by independent academic research and relevant course material. You will submit 1) a design rationale of 1000 words explaining the features of your design, your design process, and how your design relates to a social, political or cultural issue. You will also create 2) an audiovisual media product demonstrating the features of your design, such as diagrams or product mockups, alongside a process walkthrough. Further details will be made available in a task sheet and rubric on Blackboard. Any use of generative AI must be acknowledged and explained, and you must show evidence of having created assets WITHOUT the use of generative AI to fulfil the criteria.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
30/10/2026 4:00 pm

Consultations will take place in workshops in Week 11 and 12.
assessments.task_description:
To explore your speculative media technology from the Media Proposal and Design Draft, you will create a series of digital media artefacts (photos, advertisements, videos, social media content, packaging designs etc.) about the media technology you created for the media proposal. You will also present a reflection (1000 words) explaining the context and design of the artefacts. These artefacts should be chosen to demonstrate how the media technology you created for the first assessment was marketed and received by society, exploring the consequences of the technology through social, political and cultural lenses. Your own creation of assets (photos, drawings, raw video etc) will be highly valued. As with the Media Proposal and Design Draft, your Media Portfolio must address the course driving questions: How do digital media technologies shape society? How are they in turn shaped by society? 

The final assessment consists of four components:

  1. Media Portfolio
  2. Critical Reflection
  3. Process Walkthrough
  4. Consultation (in person)


Media Portfolio: Students are expected to hand in a media portfolio that builds on their designs from the Media Proposal and Design Draft. The portfolio should explore social, cultural or political issues through audio-visual means, bringing strong understandings of media theory to life. The content of the portfolio should be discussed with your tutor in advance of submission, but must have been created using the Adobe suite unless permission is received from your tutor. It can include a wide variety of mediums and forms, but all media must be edited and polished with attention to typography and other design aspects, and incorporating strong prose or copy. If internet-sourced imagery is used, it has been cited and substantially altered in software programs alongside original material. The project should not rely on AI produced imagery in a way that jeopardises the project's originality and the student's demonstration of advanced media production skills.

Critical Reflection: You will write 1000 words from your own perspective that explains how the portfolio answers the driving question of the unit. How does the portfolio use design to speculate about the future and critically speak to the present? You must do this with reference to course readings and materials and your own academic research. This should be submitted alongside the portfolio. You must also respond to feedback from your tutor in the consultation.

Process Walkthrough: You must submit a document explaining your creative process, documenting how you made the media artefacts you have included in your portfolio. You must clearly document your step by step process with screenshots, detailing the software you have used to create the artefacts and the technical skills you have demonstrated in doing so.

Consultation: You will be assigned a week (last two weeks of workshops) to attend a short consultation with your tutor about your project. You will be asked questions by your tutor that may ask you to explain the media technology, the media artefacts you've created, and how they speak to a social, political or cultural issue with reference to academic research and course materials. You will be given verbal feedback from your tutor to incorporate into your final portfolio submission. Attending the consultation is a hurdle requirement to pass this assessment item and is thus mandatory. If you are ill on your scheduled consultation day you must contact staff before the scheduled workshop and will need to arrange a medical certificate.

Further details will be made available in a task sheet and rubric on Blackboard. Any use of generative AI must be acknowledged and explained, and you must show evidence of having created assets WITHOUT the use of generative AI to fulfil the criteria. As the consultations are secure, there is no allowed generative AI during the consultation, and you will need to demonstrate your comprehension of your portfolio without the assistance of generative AI.

Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment:

All consultations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  

Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  

Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of:

  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application
